action.reasoning: Observation: The best Quickdraw accuracy achieved so far was 4.71% with a 2-epoch full DINOv2 fine-tune (iteration 10). The 15-epoch DINOv2 run collapsed to 2.31% due to catastrophic forgetting, and the 10-epoch frozen-backbone run only reached 0.75%, confirming that (a) some backbone adaptation is crucial, but (b) aggressive tuning destroys OOD generalization. The old best checkpoint was overwritten, so I must train a new strong model. Analysis: I have not yet tried CLIP ViT-B/16, which is highlighted as the backbone used by CLIP-DG papers and may transfer better to sketch-like Quickdraw due to its diverse web-scale pretraining. Additionally, all prior runs used a simple Linear classification head; adding a small MLP head (hidden→GELU→Dropout→out) is a standard fine-tuning recipe for ViTs and often yields a few points of improvement. Decision: (1) Update model.py to use an MLP head for all ViT backbones (DINOv2, ViT-B/16 AugReg, CLIP) while keeping ResNet-50 unchanged. (2) Run a conservative 3-epoch full fine-tuning of CLIP ViT-B/16 with lr=1e-4, batch size 256, single GPU (stable), domain-balanced sampling, strong augmentations (RandAugment + RandomGrayscale p=0.5), and EMA. (3) Immediately submit the resulting checkpoint for evaluation to measure whether CLIP outperforms the old DINOv2 baseline.
